steps.m: A mysterious letter opening this chapter (followed by وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ)
steps.n: This is one of the special letters that opens some chapters of the Quran. While its exact meaning is known only to Allah, it draws attention to the miraculous nature of the Quran that follows. The long vowel mark (ٓ) shows it's pronounced as a full letter name 'Qāf' rather than just the sound 'q'.
Connected root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb

steps.m: recitation, that which is read abundantly
steps.n: Changes verb 'read' into noun meaning 'that which is read/recited frequently'
Adds kasra to show this is owned by/connected to المَجِيدِ (the Glorious)
Adds ال to make the noun definite
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect with previous word/phrase
The root letters form the base meaning of glory and nobility
Changed from root letters to an adjective by adding the vowels 'a' and 'i'
Added ي to show this is a strong or permanent quality
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - 'the' instead of 'a'
The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to something before it

This is a particle that introduces a statement that contrasts with what was expected. Here it introduces the disbelievers' surprising reaction of amazement at receiving a warner from among themselves.
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form showing someone feeling amazement
Added وا to show multiple people were amazed
Added ا to lengthen the final sound

steps.m: that (connects 'they were amazed' with 'a warner came')
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it shows what amazed them - the fact that a warner came to them.
Basic past tense verb pattern with fatha marks

steps.m: he came to them (referring to the disbelievers mentioned later as ٱلْكٰفِرُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُم at the end to show who he came to. This connects to the disbelievers (ٱلْكٰفِرُونَ) mentioned later in the ayah.

steps.m: to warn or alert someone about danger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowel to kasra to show who does the warning
Added double damma at end to show it's indefinite (a warner)
Starting with the basic preposition that shows something coming from somewhere else
The dot under the first letter (م) disappears and a small double mark (shadda) is added on top because it comes after a pause
Added هُمْ at the end to show this means 'them' (referring to the disbelievers mentioned in the verse)
Changed from basic root to past tense 'he said' by adding fathas. The middle letter و becomes ا in this pattern.

steps.m: so he said (after they were amazed)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened right after they were amazed

steps.m: to reject, deny, cover up truth

steps.n: Changes from basic root meaning 'reject/deny' to mean 'someone who rejects/denies' - like changing 'deny' to 'denier'. This person is doing the speaking (قَالَ) in this verse.
Adding ون makes it plural for more than two men

steps.m: the ones who reject/deny (plural, subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific, and َ shows this is the subject doing the action of saying
This is the basic form that points to something
Added ذا to make it specifically point to something masculine and nearby

steps.m: this (masculine, pointing to عَجِيبٌ - something strange)
steps.n: Added the alif mark (ٰ) and fatha marks to complete the word. In this ayah, it's pointing to something the disbelievers found strange.

steps.m: to be a thing, to exist

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukoon on middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'thing'. The hamza at the end comes from the root letter أ.
Added tanween damma to show this word is indefinite and is describing what 'this' (هٰذَا) is in the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters and changed middle vowel to kasra to create an adjective showing intensity
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing the word شَىْءٌ (thing) in the ayah
This is a basic word that means 'when' - it's used to talk about time, like saying 'when something happens'

Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question, like adding 'is' at the start of an English sentence to make it a question. The extra ء (hamza) is added to help with pronunciation when two vowels meet.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: we died (in أَءِذَا مِتْنَا - when we died)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is part of a question about what happens after death, coming after أَءِذَا (when?).
Added basic past tense vowels to make the simplest form of the verb
Adding نَّا shows 'we' did the action in the past
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to the previous phrase 'When we die' (أَءِذَا مِتْنَا)
Shows what something is made of or its basic substance
Added the accusative case ending because this word describes what 'we' (in كُنَّا) will become.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) above the ذ
Adding لِ changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far away
Adding كَ shows you're talking to one person, like saying 'that thing I'm telling you about'
Transforms verb root into noun meaning 'return' by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on second. This creates a noun from the action.

steps.m: return, coming back (describes ذٰلِكَ as subject)
steps.n: Added double damma (tanween) at the end to show this is the subject. In this ayah, this word describes 'that' (ذٰلِكَ), saying 'that return' is what is far-fetched.
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb showing something becoming distant
Changed the verb into an adjective by adding ي between second and third root letters
Added tanween (double damma) to make the word indefinite
This is a particle that adds emphasis or certainty to the verb that follows it (عَلِمْنَا). When used with past tense verbs like here, it shows that something has definitely happened or is completed.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Adding نا changes it from 'he knew' to 'we knew'
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' that connects to the verb عَلِمْنَا (we know) before it. In this ayah, it introduces what Allah knows about - specifically what the earth takes from them.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Adding تَ at start changes it from past to present tense
The root letters were given vowels (fatha and sukun) to make the basic word for 'earth'. This earth is doing the action of shrinking/diminishing.
Added definite article 'al' and damma for nominative case
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to (them)
Added vowels to make the word usable. This is a special word called a 'location word' that shows where or with whom something is.
Added conjunction وَ (and) at beginning of word

steps.m: and with us (connected to كِتٰبٌ - a book is with us)
steps.n: Added first person plural pronoun suffix نَا (us/our). This word connects to كِتٰبٌ (book) in the next part of the verse, showing that we have a book.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he wrote' to 'writing' by changing the vowels inside the word - this makes it a thing instead of an action
Added an alif in the middle to change from 'writing' to 'something written' (like a book)
Added double dammah to make it 'a book' instead of 'the book'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The ي in the middle shows this is someone/something that does this action intensively or as their main quality
The ٌۢ at the end shows this is describing an indefinite noun (كِتٰبٌ/book) in the subject position

This is a small word that means 'rather' or 'actually'. It's used here to introduce a statement that corrects or contradicts what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it introduces how people denied the truth when it came to them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning intensive - changing from simple denial to emphatic rejection
The وا ending shows this is a group doing the action in the past. In this ayah, they are specifically rejecting الْحَقِّ (the truth)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
The second letter gets a shadda (double mark) to make it stronger, and we remove the last vowel to make it a noun instead of a verb
Adding ال makes it specific - like saying 'the truth' instead of just 'truth'
Adding بِ at the start means 'with', and we add ِ at the end because بِ requires it
This is the basic negation particle that refers to past events
Adding ّا changes this from negation to showing sequence - one thing happened right after another. Here it connects 'they denied' with 'it came to them'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This word is part of what they denied in كَذَّبُوا (they denied).
Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the action (them)
Added damma and sukun to show proper pronunciation of the pronoun 'they'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before. In this ayah, it shows the result of them rejecting the truth when it came to them.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it connects to the word أَمْرٍ (matter/affair) to show they are 'in' a confused state.
The ى with ٓ (alif) is a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning. It's still pronounced as 'fee'.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter. This makes it mean the result of commanding - which is an order/matter.

steps.m: a matter (that is affected by فِي)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is any matter (not a specific one) and that it follows the word في (in/within)

steps.m: to mix up or be in confusion

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he was confused'
Added ي between second and third root letters to make it into a descriptive word
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this describes the word أَمْرٍ (matter/affair) in the ayah
The م gets doubled (shadda) because it comes after the word فِي - this is a common rule in Arabic pronunciation
This particle turns a statement into a question, like adding a question mark in English
Adding فَ shows this question follows from something before it
Adding لَ turns the question into 'have they not?' - making it negative
Adding مْ completes the negative past meaning. This combination (لَمْ) makes the next verb (يَنظُرُوٓا۟) refer to the past, like saying 'did not look' instead of 'do not look'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Transforms past tense to present tense 3rd person masculine singular
Changed from 'he looks' to 'they look' - the و and ا at the end show multiple people are looking instead of just one person
A preposition showing direction - in this ayah it connects 'they look' (يَنظُرُوٓا۟) with 'the sky' (ٱلسَّمَآءِ) to show what they are looking towards

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated
steps.n: The basic concept of height or elevation
Transforms the root meaning of 'rising up' into the physical sky/heaven above us
Added ال to make it specific. The س gets a shadda because it comes after ال.
Added kasra at the end to show it's connected to the word فَوْقَهُمْ (above them). The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's a long vowel.
Added fatha and sukun to form the basic noun showing position. The و here is part of the root, not an addition.

steps.m: above them (referring to the sky السَّمَآءِ)
steps.n: Added هم ('them') at the end to show who/what is being above. The هم refers back to the sky mentioned earlier.
This is a question word that always asks about the way something happens
Adding vowel marks to make the word pronounceable
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
The ى changes back to يْ because it's no longer at the end of the word (we're going to add something after it)
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action

steps.m: we built it (referring to السَّمَآءِ/the sky)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show what was built (the sky). The alif in نَا changes to ٰ because another suffix is added.
Basic past tense form
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added shadda to show intense or thorough beautification
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added و with fatha to connect this action with the previous statement

steps.m: and we beautified it (referring to السَّمَاء/the sky)
steps.n: Added ها to show the sky is what was beautified
Basic interrogative/negative particle that can mean either 'what' or 'not' depending on context
Added و at beginning to connect this phrase to the previous one about 'above them'
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: for it/to it (feminine) (referring to السَّمَآءِ - the sky)
steps.n: Added ها which shows this is about something feminine (in this case, the sky which was mentioned before)
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects 'cracks/gaps' (فُرُوجٍ) to 'the sky' (السَّمَآءِ), showing the cracks are in/from the sky.
The root meaning changes to refer to a specific body part
Changes to plural form to show multiple openings
Added tanween kasra to show this refers to any gaps or openings, specifically referring to gaps in the sky being discussed in this ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a proper noun
The definite article ال makes the noun specific/definite
Added وَ (and) at the beginning and َ at the end to show it's the object of the verb مَدَدْنٰهَا (We spread it out)
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'extended' in the past tense

steps.m: we extended (acts on الأرض - the earth)
steps.n: Added نا to show 'we' did the action. This verb acts on الأرض (the earth) which comes before it in the ayah.

steps.m: we extended it (referring to الأرض - the earth)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to الأرض (the earth) mentioned earlier. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's elongated.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding hamza at beginning makes the action more direct or forceful. The word رَوٰسِىَ (mountains) is what was thrown/placed.
Subject pronoun نا indicates 'we' as the doer. This is a complete verb form and is not related to another word through case endings.
Conjunction و connects to previous statement
This is a particle that shows location or position
The word 'في' (in) gets 'ها' attached to mean 'in it', pointing back to 'earth' mentioned earlier

steps.m: to be firmly fixed or anchored

steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun for something that is fixed in place
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple mountains
The word for 'mountains' is written slightly differently but means the same thing - it's the object (thing receiving the action) of 'we cast'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Adding 'أ' transforms to causative meaning 'to make something grow'. This is a verb, not a noun, so it doesn't have nominative, genitive or accusative case.
Added نا to show 'we' did the action. This is a verb, not a noun, so it doesn't have case endings.
Adding conjunction 'وَ' means 'and'. The small ۢ mark (called قلقلة) shows a special pronunciation of ن.
This is one of the basic prepositions in Arabic that shows where something is located
The هَا at the end refers back to الْأَرْضَ (the earth) which was mentioned earlier in the ayah. This shows that something is being placed in/on the earth.

steps.m: from/of (connects كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ to what came before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing the starting point or source of something. In this ayah, it connects 'every beautiful pair' to the previous statement about what Allah made grow.
Added a shadda (double letter mark) on the ل and damma on the ك to show this means 'every single one' without exception
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word connects to زَوْجٍ (pair/kind) that comes after it, like saying 'every kind' or 'each pair'

steps.m: to pair or join things together
steps.n: This is the basic concept of pairing or joining things together

steps.m: a pair or type (of something beautiful)
steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun referring to one of a pair or type
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is describing an indefinite type of something beautiful, connecting to the word بَهِيجٍ (beautiful) that comes after it in the ayah
Added ي between the second and third letters with vowel marks to create an adjective describing something that has this quality
Added tanwin kasra to show this word describes زَوْجٍ (type/kind) in the ayah
Connected root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changes from 'he saw' to 'you (feminine) see' by adding ت
Changed from verb 'to see' into a noun meaning 'something that helps you see/understand' by adding ة at the end

steps.m: as a means of insight (for عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ - the returning servant)
steps.n: Added tanween (the -an sound) to show this word describes how something serves as a reminder or lesson
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make it a noun
Added ى with alif at end to make it mean 'a reminder' (something that helps you remember)
Added conjunction و to connect this word with تَبْصِرَةً (insight) that comes before it in the ayah
The doubled ل with shadda shows completeness, making it mean 'all' or 'every' without exception
Adding لِ shows this word connects to عَبْدٍ (servant) that comes after it, showing 'for every servant'
Changed the root letters for 'serve/worship' into a noun meaning 'servant' by adding vowel marks. Shows who belongs to كُلِّ (every) in this ayah.
Added tanween (double kasra) at the end to show this is any servant, not a specific one. The word is still owned by كُلِّ (every).
Added مُ at start and ِ in middle to show who does the action. The و becomes ي because it's between two kasras.

steps.m: one who constantly turns back (in repentance)
steps.n: Adding shadda on م makes the meaning stronger, showing this is a constant or repeated action

steps.m: one who constantly turns back (in repentance) (possessed by عَبْدٍ)
steps.n: Adding ٍ shows this describes عَبْدٍ (servant) from earlier in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled ز shows this happened multiple times or gradually
The نا shows this is 'we' doing the action. This is a complete verb form that doesn't have a case ending.

steps.m: and we sent down repeatedly/gradually (the blessed rain from السَّمَآءِ)
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous statement. This verb describes sending down blessed rain (مَآءً مُّبٰرَكًا) from the sky (السَّمَآءِ)
Basic preposition showing source or origin
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word السَّمَآءِ (the sky). This combination means 'from the sky'
Changed from root letters to past tense verb meaning 'it rose up' by adding vowels
Added اء to make it a noun meaning 'sky'
Added 'the' (ال) at the start. The ل joins into the س making it stronger (with shadda)
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by مِنَ (from) in the phrase مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ (from the sky)
The ا is added after م to make the common noun pattern for water

steps.m: water (object of نَزَّلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: Adding tanween (ً) shows this is the object receiving the action of 'sending down' in the phrase 'We sent down water'

steps.m: to bless, to make grow, to increase in goodness

steps.n: Shows something receiving blessing. In this ayah, it describes the rain (مَآءً) as being blessed, connecting to how it makes plants grow.
Added emphasis by doubling the م with a shadda
Changed alif to alif with bar (ٰ) - this is just a writing variation
Added tanween to show this describes the water (مَآءً) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the verb causative - showing that someone/something caused the growing to happen
Adding نَا at the end shows that 'we' did the action

steps.m: and so we made (something) grow (connected to مَآءً مُّبٰرَكًا)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning shows this action happened as a result of the blessed water mentioned before
Added kasra to the letter ب to help with pronunciation
Added هِ at the end to show what the preposition 'with' refers to. Here it refers back to the water (مَآءً) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added a small alif (ۦ) after ه to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to cover or hide something completely

steps.n: Adding fatha vowels creates the basic past tense verb form

steps.m: a garden (because it covers the ground with plants)
steps.n: Forms feminine noun meaning 'garden' (because gardens cover/conceal the ground)
Changed from one garden to many gardens
Added the tanwin kasra (-in sound) at the end to show this word is receiving an action in the sentence
Doubling the middle letter adds emphasis to the basic meaning
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to the previous word جَنَّاتٍ (gardens), showing that Allah grew both gardens and grains
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the word to show the thing that was harvested rather than the person doing the harvesting

steps.m: the harvested (crops that were cut down)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it follows حَبَّ (grain) in the ayah
Root meaning 'separate' became a simple noun meaning 'palm tree' - like many plant names in Arabic that come from descriptive roots
Added ال (al) at the start to mean 'the', which made the ن get a shadda (double mark)
Added 'and' at the beginning to connect it to what came before. The alif in 'al' changes to a smaller form (ٱ) when it comes after و

steps.m: and the palm tree (object being described by بَاسِقٰتٍ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being described as tall/lofty (بَاسِقٰتٍ) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Forms active participle describing something that is tall. This word describes النَّخْلَ (palm trees) in this ayah.

steps.m: tall ones (feminine plural, describing النَّخْلَ palm trees)
steps.n: Adding feminine plural suffix 'ات' to make it refer to multiple feminine entities

steps.m: tall ones (feminine plural, describing النَّخْلَ palm trees)
steps.n: The word gets dots under the last letter (tanwin) to show it's describing multiple tall things in a general way
Added emphasis to the preposition 'for' by adding a shadda, making it stronger

steps.m: surely for it/her (referring to النَّخْلَ - the palm trees)
steps.n: Added the feminine pronoun ها which refers back to the palm trees (النَّخْلَ) mentioned earlier in the verse
Changes the verb into a noun for the emerging flower cluster of a date palm, which rises and emerges from the tree
Adds tanween damma to show this is the subject being described as 'arranged in layers' (نَّضِيدٌ) in the ayah

steps.m: to arrange in layers or stack neatly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an adjective describing how something is arranged. This describes how the fruit clusters (طَلْعٌ) are arranged on the palm trees.
Adds tanween dammah (double damma) to show this is an indefinite adjective

steps.m: perfectly arranged in layers (describing طَلْعٌ/fruit clusters)
steps.n: The ن gets doubled (shadda) to connect smoothly with the word before it, but keeps the same meaning of 'arranged in layers'

steps.m: to provide or sustain with resources

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'provide' into the noun 'provision' by adding vowel marks

steps.m: abundant provision (for للعباد - for the servants)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the abundance of the provision. This word relates to للعباد (for the servants) in the ayah.

steps.m: as an abundant provision (for للعباد - for the servants)
steps.n: Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this is the object being provided
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from singular verb to plural noun showing multiple worshippers
Added لِ (meaning 'for/to') and ال (meaning 'the') to make it 'for the servants'. The ـِ ending shows it's being acted upon
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'to live'
Added hamza prefix to create causative meaning (making something live)
Added نَا to show 'we' did the action

steps.m: and we gave life (to بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا - a dead land)
steps.n: Added و to connect with previous phrase. This verb acts on 'a dead land' mentioned later in the ayah.
Added kasra to the letter ب to show it's a preposition meaning with/by
Added هِ at the end to show this refers back to the provision (رِّزْقًا) mentioned earlier
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Root letters put together with marks (fatha-sukun) to make the basic word for 'land'
Added ة at the end to show one specific instance
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb 'we revived' (أَحْيَيْنَا)
Changes from abstract 'death' to describing something that is dead
Added shadda for emphasis to show complete state of death
Added tanween alif to show this word is describing the land (بَلْدَة) mentioned in the ayah
Starting with the basic demonstrative word ذا (this). The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an alif khanjariya
Adding لِ changes the meaning from 'this' (near) to 'that' (far)
Adding كَ at the end completes the demonstrative word 'that'

steps.m: like that/similarly (referring to how Allah brings forth the dead on the Day of Resurrection, just as He brings dead land back to life)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the beginning changes it to mean 'like that' or 'similarly'. This word connects the previous example of bringing dead land to life with the upcoming mention of resurrection (الْخُرُوجُ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: going out/exit (as an action or event)
steps.n: Changed the pattern to show the action itself rather than someone doing it
Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject. In this context, it refers specifically to coming out of graves for resurrection.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger - it's not just lying, but completely rejecting something as false

steps.m: she strongly denied/rejected (the people before قَبْلَهُمْ)
steps.n: The ت at the end shows this was done by a feminine subject (referring to each nation mentioned after this verb: قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ)

steps.m: to be in front of, to precede

steps.n: Changes from root to basic noun meaning 'before'
Added هُمْ at the end to mean 'them', referring to the people being talked about. Also added َ (fatha) before هُمْ because it's needed when connecting these parts.
Added vowels to make a noun meaning 'people' - comes from the idea of a group that stands together or establishes something
The ُ at the end shows this word is doing the action in the sentence. Here it refers to the people of Noah who denied/rejected the message.
This is the proper name of the prophet Noah. The ُ (damma) on ن helps with pronunciation.
The ٍ (tanween kasra) at the end shows this word belongs to or describes something else. Here it shows these are 'the people of Noah'.

steps.m: to be close to someone, keep company

steps.n: Added letters to show who does the action, like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'
Changed the word to show many companions instead of just one
Changed the 'a' sound to be longer and added U sound at end to show this word is doing something in the sentence
Added 'and' at the beginning to connect it with what came before

steps.m: to dig deeply or bury something

steps.n: The doubled س shows this isn't just any hole, but specifically a deep well that was dug

steps.m: the deep well (possessed by أَصْحٰبُ - people of)
steps.n: Adding ٱل makes it refer to a specific well. The ِ at the end shows it belongs to or is connected to the 'people of' (أَصْحٰبُ). The ر gets doubled because it comes right after ٱل.

steps.m: Thamud (name of an ancient Arabian tribe)
steps.n: This is a proper noun naming an ancient Arabian people mentioned in the Quran. They were the people to whom Prophet Salih was sent.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this word is the subject doing the action of denying/rejecting in this verse
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it in the verse (the people of Noah and the companions of the well)

steps.m: to return repeatedly, to be customary

steps.n: This is the name of an ancient Arab tribe, coming from the root meaning 'to return'. The name is spelled with just 3 letters: ع-ا-د.
Added و meaning 'and' at the start to connect it with فِرْعَوْنُ (Pharaoh) in a list. The ٌ (tanween) at the end shows it's a complete subject.
This is a borrowed word from ancient Egyptian, so it doesn't have an Arabic root
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence, like وَعَادٌ before it
Added وَ (wa) at the beginning to connect this word with وَعَادٌ (and 'Ad) before it
Changes from the root meaning 'brotherhood' to make the simple word 'brother' by adding the vowel mark fatha
Changes to plural form by modifying first vowel and adding وان pattern
Adds و at beginning to mean 'and', connecting it to previous words (عَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ)
Adds ُ to show this is a subject, like عَادٌ and فِرْعَوْنُ before it
This is a proper noun (name) borrowed from Hebrew, so it doesn't have an Arabic root
Added damma (ُ) on first letter to show proper pronunciation
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word belongs to or describes إِخْوٰنُ (people/followers) mentioned before it
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to accompany'
Changed to show the person who does the action
Changed to plural form to show multiple companions
Changed to show this word is doing the action (added ُ at end), with a slight spelling change in the middle
Added و at start to connect this with previous phrase
This is a standalone word without a clear root. The hamza has a fatha and there's a sukun on the ي to show proper pronunciation.
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific. The hamza on the alif becomes a wasl hamza (ٱ) because it comes after the definite article.

steps.m: of the thicket (possessed by أَصْحٰبُ - companions)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by أَصْحٰبُ (companions), forming the phrase 'companions of the thicket'.
Root meaning 'stand' became a noun for 'people' by adding vowels - like how people stand together as a group
Added the connecting word 'and' at the beginning
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in كَذَّبَ (denied/rejected)
While this word became a title, it comes from the concept of following/succeeding
The root 'follow' becomes a royal title 'Tubba' by adding damma and doubling the middle letter
The tanwin kasra shows this word belongs to or describes قَوْمُ (people), making the phrase 'people of Tubba'
Added damma on first letter and doubled the second letter to create a word meaning 'all' or 'every'. This word is the subject of كَذَّبَ (denied/rejected) in this ayah.
Added double damma at the end to show this is the main subject doing the action in the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning intensive and changes it from just lying to actively rejecting or denying something. In this ayah, it shows the rejection of the messengers (رُّسُلَ).
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (to deliver) to a noun (messenger) by adding و
The word changed from one messenger to many messengers by changing the middle vowel from 'u' to 'u' and dropping the long 'oo' sound
Added definite article and doubled the ر with shadda
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb كَذَّبَ (denied/rejected)

steps.m: to be true, to be proven true

steps.n: The root letters were joined together and vowel marks were added to make it a past tense verb meaning 'it became true'

steps.m: and thus became true (consequence of كَذَّبَ 'they denied')
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this was the result of them denying the messengers mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to warn or promise (usually about future consequences)

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to warn' into a noun meaning 'a warning/threat'
The word gets a kasra (i) sound at the end to show it belongs to Allah, meaning 'My threat' instead of just 'a threat'. It connects to the word حَقَّ (was fulfilled) to mean 'My threat was fulfilled'
Adding vowels to make past tense verb showing a state
Added فَ to connect this statement with what came before
Added أَ to turn the statement into a question

steps.m: did We then become weary/unable? (connected to بِٱلْخَلْقِ ٱلْأَوَّلِ - with the first creation)
steps.n: Added نَا ('We') at the end to show Allah is speaking about Himself. Connected to 'the first creation' in the verse

steps.m: to create or form something new

steps.n: Root meaning 'create' becomes a noun meaning 'creation' by adding the vowels َ (fatha) and ْ (sukun). Like changing 'create' to 'creating'.
Added ال to make it specific, like saying 'the creation' instead of just any creation

steps.m: by/with the creation (connected to عَيِينَا - were we tired)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to show 'by/with' and ِ at end because it follows بِ. This shows how the creation relates to being tired in the phrase 'Were We tired by/with the creation?'
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic word
The middle letter is doubled to make the meaning stronger
The definite article ال is added to make the word specific/definite
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to الخلق (creation) in the ayah

steps.m: but rather (correcting the previous statement about being tired with creation)
steps.n: This is a word that shows disagreement with what came before. Here it's saying 'No, that's not right about being tired. But rather...' and then gives the real situation.
This is a pronoun that refers to a group of males or a mixed group. In this ayah, it's the subject of the phrase that means 'they are in confusion'.
Basic preposition showing location or state. In this ayah, it introduces where/how they are - in confusion (فِى لَبْسٍ)
Added ى at the end which is standard for this preposition when not connected to the next word

steps.m: to mix or confuse things together
confusion (a state of being mixed up)
steps.n: Changed from verb 'to mix up' into noun 'confusion' by adding fatha and sukun marks
The word follows فِى (in) in the ayah, so it takes kasratain to show this relationship
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects the words لَبْسٍ (confusion) and خَلْقٍ (creation) to show they are confused about a new creation.
Added shadda to strengthen the م sound because it comes after another م sound in the previous word (هُمْ)
Added sukun to show the ن has no vowel sound after it

steps.m: to create or form something new
creation (the act or result of creating)
steps.n: Changes from basic root meaning 'create' into a noun meaning 'the act of creating' or 'creation'

steps.m: a creation (being acted upon in فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this word is indefinite and is being acted upon. In this ayah, it's being affected by the phrase فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ (in doubt about).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (making something new) to a describing word meaning 'new'. Like changing 'renew' to 'new'.

steps.m: new (describing خَلْقٍ/creation, showing it follows مِنْ/from)
steps.n: The tanween kasra ending shows this word follows مِنْ (from) in the phrase مِنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (from a new creation)
This is the base particle that shows emphasis or certainty
Added ل at the beginning to add extra emphasis, like saying 'surely' instead of just 'indeed'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to create from nothing, to make something new
steps.n: Basic meaning of creation or making something exist
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the last vowel to sukun (ْ) to prepare for attaching نا. This is like adding glue to connect two pieces.
Adding نا at the end changes it from 'he created' to 'we created'

steps.m: to be close and familiar with others
steps.n: Root relates to human companionship and sociability
Root meaning 'being social' became a noun meaning all humans (since humans are social beings)
Changes from general 'mankind' to mean a single human being
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the human being (as the object being created in وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا)
steps.n: Added fatḥa at the end to show this is the object being created in the phrase 'and We have created'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he knew' to 'we know' by adding نَ at the start to mean 'we'
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it in the ayah
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' that connects to the verb نَعْلَمُ (we know). In this ayah, it refers to whatever thoughts or whispers occur in a person's mind.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and show past tense
Changed from past to present tense and made it feminine
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/by

steps.m: with/by it (referring to what وَسْوَسَ whispers)
steps.n: Added ه (meaning 'it') to show what/who the action is done with - like saying 'with IT' instead of just 'with'
Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation guide
Root relates to self, soul, or essence
Changed letters from separated to connected form and added vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'soul' or 'self'. This word is doing the whispering (subject) in this ayah.

steps.m: his self (the one doing the whispering)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is doing the action, and هُۥ to show it belongs to him
Added vowel marks to show this is a subject pronoun. This word is the subject (doer) of أَقْرَبُ (closer) in this ayah.
Added conjunction و at the beginning to connect this clause with the previous statement.
Root relates to nearness or proximity
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb meaning 'was near'
Changed to comparative form meaning 'nearer/closer'. In this ayah, it describes how Allah is closer to humans than their jugular vein.
Basic preposition indicating direction or destination
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns

steps.m: to him/it (connected to نَعْلَمُ - we know)
steps.n: Added pronoun ه (him/it) at the end. In this ayah, it refers back to الإنسان (the human) and connects to the verb نَعْلَمُ (we know).
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. In this ayah, it connects the word حَبْلِ (vein) to أَقْرَبُ (closer), showing that Allah is closer to humans than their jugular vein.
Root relates to rope or binding
Added vowel marks to transform the root into a noun meaning the thing used for tying
Added kasra at the end to show this rope is connected to the next word (الوَرِيدِ meaning 'jugular vein')
Root relates to arriving or reaching
The ي between ر and د changes this from an action into a noun for the path blood flows through. The َ (fatha) on و and ِ (kasra) on ر show this is a noun not a verb.

steps.m: the jugular vein (connected to حَبْلِ/cord in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ (the) at start and ِ at end because this word follows مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'closer than (مِنْ) the jugular vein'. The jugular vein is being compared to show how close Allah is to humans.

steps.m: when (referring to a specific time in the past, introducing يَتَلَقَّى)
steps.n: This is a time particle that introduces a past event. Here it sets up when the two receivers (angels) receive/take record.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The ت at the beginning and doubled ق show this is about receiving something from another source repeatedly or with effort

steps.m: he receives/is receiving (referring to the angel who records deeds)
steps.n: The يَ at the beginning shows this is happening in the present/ongoing. This refers to how the angels are constantly receiving and recording our deeds.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding مُ prefix and kasra ِ on middle letter creates active participle pattern showing the doer
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to show doing the action a lot
Adding ت after first letter shows doing action to/for oneself
Changes from one person to two people receiving (dual form)

steps.m: the two who receive intensely/repeatedly (referring to the two angels)
steps.n: The ال makes it specific - referring to the two angels mentioned in this verse who receive and record our deeds
This is the basic preposition showing movement away from something or about something
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the next word ٱلْيَمِينِ (the right side)

steps.m: to be on the right side or direction

steps.n: Added ال to make it definite. The ِ ending shows it follows the word عَنِ (from/on) in the ayah
Added kasra at the end because it's connected to the next word الشِّمَالِ (the left)
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added vowel marks to create the noun meaning 'left side' - this word comes from the root meaning of 'gathering' because the left hand gathers things toward itself

steps.m: the left side (direction from which an angel sits)
steps.n: Added ال (the) at the beginning which causes the ش to get a shadda because it's a 'sun letter'. The ِ at the end shows this word comes after عَنِ (from/on) in the ayah.

steps.m: to sit or remain in a place

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an adjective describing someone who sits or remains in place, specifically as a watcher or observer in this context

steps.m: a watcher (referring to an angel who records deeds)
steps.n: Adds tanween dammah to show this is describing one of the angels mentioned in the ayah who sits and watches to record deeds
Added fatha to show the basic pronunciation of the word. This is a question/condition word that can mean 'what' or 'any' depending on context.

steps.m: not any (followed by يَلْفِظُ - he utters)
steps.n: Added shadda (double م) to make it negative. In this ayah, it works with يَلْفِظُ (he utters) to mean 'he does not utter'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb

Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start, 2) putting sukun on first root letter, 3) putting kasra on second root letter, and 4) adding damma at end. This word describes what the 'ready observer' (رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ) is watching.
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects to قَوْلٍ (word/saying) to show what is being spoken.
The kasra (ِ) under the م shows this word connects to what comes after it. In this ayah, it helps explain that no word/saying (قَوْلٍ) comes from someone except that there are watchers present.
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
Changed from action of speaking to the thing that is spoken
The tanween at the end shows this is any general word or saying, not a specific one. In this ayah, it refers to any word that a person utters.

steps.m: except (what follows is different from لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ)
steps.n: This is an exception particle that shows what comes after it is different from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it connects the words before it (مَّا يَلْفِظُ مِن قَوْلٍ - 'he does not utter any word') with what comes after (لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ - 'except that with him is an observer ready')
This is a preposition showing location or closeness to something
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns

steps.m: with him, near him (watching over قَوْلٍ)
steps.n: The هِ shows this relates to 'him'. In this ayah, it refers to being near/with someone's speech (قَوْلٍ) with the purpose of watching over it.

steps.m: one who watches/guards intensively (describing the angel watching over deeds)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is someone's role or constant quality. In this ayah, it refers to the angel who carefully watches and records what people say.

steps.m: a watchful observer (referring to the recording angel)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this is one of the recording angels mentioned in the ayah. This angel is paired with عَتِيدٌ (ready) showing they work together to record deeds.

steps.m: to be ready, prepared, or at hand

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an adjective describing رَقِيبٌ (watcher) in this ayah

steps.m: ready/prepared (describing رَقِيبٌ as an indefinite adjective)
steps.n: Adds tanwin damma to show this is an indefinite adjective matching the case of رَقِيبٌ (watcher) that it describes
Added fatha marks and connected letters to make past tense verb. The middle letter ي becomes ا when making past tense.
Added ت with sukun at the end to show a female did the action. Also added آ (long alif) to show proper pronunciation.

steps.m: and she came (subject of سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'. This word is connected to سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ (the agony of death) which is what came.

steps.m: to be overwhelmed or dazed by something

steps.n: Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make a past-tense verb
Changed the middle vowel to sukun to turn the verb into a noun that describes the state
Added ة to show one specific instance. This word is the subject (doer) of جَآءَتْ (it came) in this ayah.
Added damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'death' instead of the action 'to die'
Added ال (written as ٱلْ) to make it specific - referring to a particular death
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by سَكْرَةُ (stupor), forming the phrase 'stupor of death'
Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Changed from a past-tense verb (was true) to a noun meaning truth, keeping the doubled letter ق
Added ال to point to a specific truth
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done - with or by means of truth
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun that points to something nearby
The alif is written in a special way (with overbar) to show elongation
Adding لِ changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far
Adding كَ completes the word by emphasizing that the thing being pointed to is far away
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. In this ayah, it connects with كُنتَ (you were) to mean 'what you were'. It's used to point to something that follows.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
The weak letter و drops out, and the vowels change to compensate. This is a common pattern with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)
Added تَ to show the subject is 'you'
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'. The ْ (sukun) shows the word stops here.

steps.m: from him/it (referring to what you were trying to avoid)
steps.n: Added هُ (him/it) to show what is being moved away from. In this ayah, it refers to what the person was trying to avoid.

steps.m: to turn away from, avoid, deviate from the right path

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a complete past tense verb
Adding تَ changes it from 'he turned away' to 'you turn away'
Added ُ at the end to show this is happening in the present/future. In this ayah, it refers to turning away from or trying to avoid the truth that was mentioned.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to passive voice to show the horn (ٱلصُّورِ) is being blown into, rather than focusing on who is doing the blowing
Added و at the beginning to connect this event with what came before it
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. Here it connects 'the horn' (ٱلصُّورِ) to the verb 'was blown' (نُفِخَ), showing where the blowing happened.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added damma to create noun meaning instrument of blowing (horn/trumpet)
Added definite article ال (written as ٱل) and shadda on ص because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the preposition فِى (in/into)
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) makes it point to something far away
Adding ك makes it masculine to match the word it's pointing to (يَوْمُ meaning 'Day')
Connected the letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun
Added damma at the end because this word is the subject (like saying 'THE Day' instead of just 'a day')
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'to warn' into noun meaning 'a warning/threat'
Added ال to make it specific (this is the Day of Warning mentioned in the Quran)
Added kasra to show this is possessed by the word يَوْمُ (day) - together meaning 'Day of Warning'
Added vowel marks and changed middle letter to long alif. This makes it past tense.
Added ت at the end to make it 'she' instead of 'he', and lengthened the vowel sound

steps.m: and she came (subject of كُلُّ نَفْسٍ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'. This word refers to كُلُّ نَفْسٍ (every soul) in the ayah.
Root 'k-l-l' becomes 'kull' meaning 'all/every' with damma and shadda to make it a noun
Added final damma to show this is the subject of the sentence. This word connects to نَفْسٍ (soul) to mean 'every soul'.
Added vowels to the root letters to make the word mean 'soul' or 'self' instead of the action of breathing
Added two dots under the last letter to show we're talking about any soul/self, not a specific one. This soul is doing the coming action.
This is a particle meaning 'with', it doesn't come from a root. Added vowel marks to show pronunciation.
Added ها which means 'her/it' at the end. This refers back to نَفْسٍ (soul) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added shadda on initial م for emphasis and smooth pronunciation when connected to previous word.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun for someone who does this action. This word works with جَآءَتْ (came) in the verse.
Added tanween to show this is 'a driver' not 'the driver', and lengthened the alif for proper pronunciation

steps.m: to witness or observe something directly

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from a past-tense verb (he witnessed) to a noun meaning someone who witnesses by adding ي - like changing 'saw' to 'seer'
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite and matches with سَآئِقٌ (driver) mentioned earlier in the verse
This is a basic emphasis word that confirms something happened
Added لَ at the beginning to make the emphasis even stronger
Added shadda on ل to strengthen its pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
The weak letter و changes to nothing and the vowels adjust according to Arabic rules for hollow verbs

steps.m: you were (referring to the person being addressed in غَفْلَةٍ/heedlessness)
steps.n: Adding تَ at the end shows this happened to 'you' in the past

steps.m: in (connects غَفْلَةٍ 'heedlessness' to what comes after)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it connects the word غَفْلَةٍ (heedlessness) to what follows, showing someone was 'in' a state of heedlessness.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun describing the state of being unaware

steps.m: in a state of unawareness (of هٰذَا/this)
steps.n: Added the kasratain ending to show this word follows فِى (in) from earlier in the verse
Basic preposition showing source or origin
The م gets doubled (gets a shadda) because it comes right after another word. This is just for easier pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.
Basic attention-drawing word that points to something
Added ذا to specify pointing to something nearby
Added alif with overbar (ٰ) and fatha marks to complete the word. In this ayah, it points to the state of heedlessness mentioned before.
Added fatha (َ) after each letter to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he did' something

steps.m: we removed a cover (from عَنكَ - from you)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action instead of 'he'. The sukun (ْ) before نَا helps connect the letters smoothly.

steps.m: so we removed a cover (from عَنكَ - from you)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what was mentioned before - that the person was in heedlessness (غَفْلَةٍ)

steps.m: from, away from (preposition related to كَشَفْنَا - we removed)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing separation or distance. Works with the verb كَشَفْنَا (we removed) to show what was removed from whom.

steps.m: from you (object of كَشَفْنَا - we removed)
steps.n: Adding كَ makes it specifically 'from you'. This shows you are the one that something was removed from. Works with كَشَفْنَا (we removed) to mean 'we removed from you'.
Root letters got vowels and alif (ا) was added to make it mean 'a cover'
Added َ to show this is being acted upon by the verb كَشَفْنَا (we uncovered). The alif is written as آ when followed by hamza.

steps.m: your cover (object of كَشَفْنَا - we uncovered)
steps.n: Added كَ to show this belongs to the person being addressed

steps.m: to see or perceive with eyes

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun meaning the ability to see
Added damma to show this is the subject of حَدِيدٌ (sharp/piercing)
Added فَ to show this follows from the previous statement about removing the cover
Added كَ to show possession (your). This complete phrase is the subject of حَدِيدٌ (sharp/piercing) in this ayah.
Basic word for 'day' with short vowels added - a fatHa (َ) on ya and a sukun (ْ) on waw to show it's a consonant

steps.m: today (time when فَبَصَرُكَ حَدِيدٌ - your sight is sharp)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific ('this day') while the َ ending shows it describes when something happens. Here it tells when 'your sight is sharp'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Forms an intensive adjective indicating strong/permanent quality
Adds tanween dammah to show this is an indefinite noun
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has و in middle). Added fatha marks to show past tense.
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this word to what came before. This word is connected to قَرِينُهُ (his companion) which comes right after it.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changes from a verb (to connect) into a noun meaning someone who connects/accompanies, like changing 'connect' to 'companion'
Adds nominative case ending to show this is the subject doing the action of saying
Adds 'his' to show whose companion it is. This companion is speaking in the ayah.
This is the basic demonstrative element meaning 'here' or 'this'

steps.m: this (pointing to something near) (subject of عَتِيدٌ)
steps.n: Added ذا to make it specifically mean 'this' for something nearby. This word is the subject of عَتِيدٌ (ready/present) in this ayah.
This is a question word used here to ask about something. In this ayah, it's asking about what is present or available, connecting to the word لَدَىَّ (with me).
This is the basic preposition meaning 'at' or 'with'. It's used to show something is physically close or in someone's possession.

steps.m: with me (referring to what is عَتِيدٌ/ready)
steps.n: Added shadda and vowel at the end to show this means 'with me'. In this ayah, it's referring to something that is ready or prepared (عَتِيدٌ).
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show this is a permanent quality rather than an action
Added tanwin (double damma) to show this is describing something that is present and prepared (in the context of this ayah, it refers to what is ready with the witness)
Adding vowels turns the root into a proper past tense verb
Adding أ at the start makes it causative - making something happen. The ى replaces ي at the end due to weak letter rules.
Adding ا at the end makes it refer to two people instead of one
Basic preposition that shows location. Here it connects the verb أَلْقِيَا (throw) with جَهَنَّمَ (Hell) to show where to throw.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly
Changed the ي to ى which is just a different way to write the same sound at the end of words

steps.m: Hell (name of the fire of punishment)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew 'Gehinnom' meaning the place of punishment in the afterlife
Added vowel marks and shadda to show correct pronunciation. This word is receiving the action of throwing (أَلْقِيَا) in this ayah.
Doubling the middle letter makes it stronger - changes from 'complete' to 'all/every'
The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb أَلْقِيَا (throw/cast). So it means '[throw] every...'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled ف and long ا make this an intensive form, showing someone who strongly or habitually disbelieves
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word is describing كُلَّ (every) in the ayah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from a past tense verb (he turned away) to a describing word (stubborn) by adding ي and changing the middle vowel
The tanween kasratin (ٍ) shows this describes كَفَّارٍ (persistent disbeliever) in the ayah that says 'Cast into Hell every persistent disbeliever, stubborn one'

steps.m: someone who always prevents good things (subject of مُّرِيبٍ)
steps.n: Added shadda (double letter) on ن and added alif to show this person does this action a lot. This describes someone mentioned in مُّرِيبٍ (suspicious/doubtful).
Adding shadda on م adds emphasis and connects this word to the previous word
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is describing an indefinite person. This person prevents الْخَيْرِ (good things).
Added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning a specific good thing or benefit

steps.m: for the good (object of مَّنَّاعٍ - preventer)
steps.n: Added لِلْ (combination of لِ meaning 'for' and الْ meaning 'the') at the beginning and ِ at the end to show this is what is being prevented, connecting it to the word مَّنَّاعٍ (preventer) before it
The ل merged with another ل to make a stronger sound, showing emphasis
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added اِعْتَ to show doing something deliberately and with force - like crossing a line on purpose

steps.m: a transgressor (described with مَّنَّاعٍ and مُّرِيبٍ)
steps.n: Changed to active participle form with مُ at start to show someone who does this action. The tanwin kasra at the end shows it's indefinite (a transgressor). This word is part of a series of descriptions with مَّنَّاعٍ (preventer) and مُّرِيبٍ (doubter).
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who or what causes the doubt

steps.m: one who causes doubt (connected to previous word)
steps.n: Added shadda on م to show connection with previous word
Added tanween kasra to show this describes the preventer (مَّنَّاعٍ) mentioned earlier in the verse
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what's being described

steps.m: the one who (placed another god with Allah)
steps.n: Adding ٱلّ at the beginning makes it point to a specific person or thing being described. Here it points to the person who committed shirk (placing another god with Allah).

steps.m: to make, put, or set something
he made/set (subject of the phrase 'who made' with ٱلَّذِى)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word follows ٱلَّذِى (who/the one who) to mean 'who made'.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is together with or alongside something else. In this ayah, it connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) to show what was placed alongside Him.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' or 'god'

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ِ shows it belongs to or connects with something else in the sentence.
Changes from basic root into noun meaning 'a god/deity'
The alif is written as a small mark above to show long 'aa' sound
Added tanween fatha (ًا) to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb جَعَلَ (made/took)
Changed the root letters into a simple adjective by adding the short vowels 'a' to mean 'another/different'
Added long alif at start to make it into a comparative form meaning 'more other/another'

steps.m: to throw or cast something away
to make throw, to cause to cast away
steps.n: Changes from doing action yourself to making someone else do it
Shows two people did the action instead of one
Adds 'him' as the object being thrown

steps.m: so throw him (command to two angels)
steps.n: Added فَ meaning 'so/then' to show this action follows from what was mentioned before. This command is given to two angels to throw the person into severe punishment.
Basic preposition showing location or containment
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to العَذَابِ (the punishment) to show location within something
Transformed to noun meaning 'punishment' using فَعَال pattern. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after the preposition في (in/into).
Added 'the' (ال) at start and 'i' sound (ِ) at end because it follows the word 'in' (في)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Changed pattern to create an intensive adjective showing a strong quality
Added 'al' for definiteness, shadda for emphasis, and kasra because it describes العَذَابِ which ends in kasra
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone (he) did the action in the past.
The middle letter و changed to ا because it's a hollow verb

steps.m: to connect or be close to
companion (someone who is close to another)
steps.n: Added vowels and ي to make a word for someone who stays close to another person
Added ُ to show this is the doer of the action in قَالَ (he said)
Added هُۥ at the end to show this is his companion
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show intensity of the nurturing quality

steps.m: our Lord (object of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Adding نا at the end means 'our' - shows we're talking about our lord. This word is the object (accusative case) of the verb قَالَ (he said) - showing what was said.
This is the basic interrogative particle meaning 'what'
Added fatha to show pronunciation
The alif is elongated with a madda mark. In this context, it functions as a negative particle that negates the verb that follows it (أَطْغَيْتُهُۥ - 'I made him transgress')
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to make someone do the action' instead of just doing it yourself
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding تُ at the end
Adding هُۥ at the end shows who was made to transgress (him)
This is the basic contrasting particle that shows something different from what was mentioned before

steps.m: but/however (connects this contrast to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement to what came before it. In this ayah, it connects to the previous statement where the companion denied leading the person astray.
Added basic past tense vowel pattern. This word is connected to قَالَ (he said) in this ayah, showing what happened in the past.
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Basic preposition showing location or position
The ى extends the sound and helps connect to the next word. In this ayah, it shows someone being 'in' a state of misguidance.

steps.m: to wander off the right path

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make it into a noun meaning the act of straying
The alif makes it into an abstract noun meaning the state or condition of being lost
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this word comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah
Added ي between the second and third root letters to show this is a strong quality. Also added vowel marks to make it an adjective.
Added tanween kasra to show this describes the state of being in error (ضَلٰلٍۭ) mentioned in the ayah
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows the basic form before the weak letter change.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
This is a negative particle that makes something not happen

Added fatha to show this is used with a command form. Here it tells people not to do something (dispute/argue). It connects directly to the word تَخْتَصِمُوا۟ (dispute/argue) to make it mean 'do not dispute.'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added pattern VIII (اِفْتَعَلَ) to show mutual action between people
Changed to present tense and second person by adding تَ prefix and changing vowels

steps.m: you (all) argue with others (in response to قَالَ لَا)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people are being addressed. This is what they are told not to do (لَا)
This is a preposition showing location or presence. The fathas are added to show it's being used as a preposition.

steps.m: with me, at my place (connected to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added ي (ya) with shadda to mean 'with me' instead of just 'with'. Like adding 'my' or 'me' to show it's personal. Connected to قَالَ (he said) to show where something was said.
This particle shows something definitely happened. Here it emphasizes that Allah definitely presented the warning.
Added وَ to connect it to what came before, showing this is a continuation of the conversation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the action more direct and transitive - something is being put forward rather than just moving forward
Adding تُ changes the subject to 'I' and adds sukun before it
This is a preposition showing direction
Added sukun before attaching pronoun suffix

steps.m: to you all (object of قَدَّمْتُ - I had presented)
steps.n: Added suffix كُم to show this is directed to multiple people. This word is receiving the action of قَدَّمْتُ (I had presented) in this ayah.

steps.m: to warn or promise (usually about consequences)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added ي and kasra to make it into a noun meaning 'the thing that is warned/promised'
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: with the warning/threat (related to قَدَّمْتُ - I had already presented)
steps.n: Added بِ meaning 'with' at the beginning and kasra at the end because it follows a preposition. This word relates to قَدَّمْتُ (I had already presented) in the ayah.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُبَدَّلُ (it is changed/altered) into 'it is not changed/altered'. In this ayah, Allah is saying that His word/statement cannot be changed.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows this is done with intensity or repeatedly
The يُ prefix shows this is happening now, and the ُ ending shows this is happening to something (passive). The word الْقَوْلُ (the word/statement) is what's being changed.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'speech'. This word is what's being talked about in the phrase 'speech is not changed'.
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject of يُبَدَّلُ (is changed)
This is a basic word showing location or closeness to something, like saying 'at' or 'near' in English

steps.m: with me (connected to يُبَدَّلُ - is changed)
steps.n: Adding the doubled ي (ىَّ) at the end shows this means 'with me'. This word connects to يُبَدَّلُ (is changed) to say 'is changed with me'
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added ما (meaning 'not') to و (meaning 'and') to make 'and not'. Works with the verb يُبَدَّلُ to say something is not changed.
The alif is written with a long mark (آ) instead of regular alif (ا). This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'I' - it doesn't come from a root since it's one of the fundamental building blocks of the language

The small circle (۠) is a special mark showing this word should be pronounced with a longer sound at the end. This emphasizes that Allah is speaking about Himself in this ayah, declaring that He is not unjust to His servants.

steps.m: to wrong, oppress, or act unjustly

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added shadda and alif to show someone who does this action intensively or habitually

steps.m: with/by one who constantly wrongs (related to أَنَا)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to mean 'with/by' and tanween at end to show indefiniteness. This describes Allah (أَنَا) saying He is not like this.
Forms the basic noun meaning 'servant' or 'slave'
Changes single 'servant' into plural 'servants/slaves' by adding ي in the middle
Makes it specific which servants we're talking about
Added 'li' at start to mean 'for/to' the servants
The letter ل is doubled to add extra emphasis, like saying 'truly for the servants'
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the basic word for 'day'
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is in the accusative case
Changed vowels to make the present tense base form. The middle ا returns to و.
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. In this ayah, Allah is speaking to Hell (جَهَنَّمَ).
This is a proper noun for Hell. It's a special word that doesn't come from Arabic root letters, but was borrowed from Hebrew 'Gehinnom'

steps.m: to Hell (being addressed in يَوْمَ نَقُولُ - the Day We will say)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'to' and َ at the end to show it's being directly addressed. This word is being spoken to in the phrase 'the Day We will say...'
This is a basic question word that starts yes/no questions

steps.m: do/does/did? (asking جَهَنَّمَ if it is full)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it's connected to the next word. In this ayah, it's asking Hell if it is full.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added ٱ at start and ت after first letter to show the action happens to the subject itself
Added تِ at the end to show this is addressing Hell (which is feminine in Arabic)
Connected the root letters with vowels. The middle root letter و changes to ا in past tense.
Changed vowels to show present tense. The middle letter returns to و.
Added تَ to show feminine subject. In this ayah, the subject is جَهَنَّمَ (Hell), which is treated as feminine in Arabic.
Added وَ (and) to connect this with the previous statement
This is a question word that turns a statement into a yes/no question. In this ayah, Allah is asking Hell if it is full.
This is a preposition that asks if there is any more space or capacity. In this ayah, it's used when Hell is being asked if it has any more room.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (to increase) to a passive participle (something that was increased) - like changing 'increase' to 'increased'
Adding shadda (double letter mark) makes the meaning stronger - like saying 'very much' or 'greatly'

steps.m: any more (asking about more in هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ - is there any more?)
steps.n: Added tanwin (two dots under) to show this word is asking 'any more?' - like asking if there's room for more of something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to passive form by adding أُ at start and changing vowels to show something was done to the subject rather than the subject doing it
Added feminine marker ت with kasra because الجنة (Paradise) is feminine

steps.m: and was brought close (referring to الجنة/Paradise)
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' to connect this clause with what came before
Added shadda to show intensity of covering, like thick garden growth

steps.m: garden (a place densely covered with vegetation)
steps.n: Adds ة to make it mean 'a garden' - a place covered by lots of plants
Added ال to make it specific - 'the' paradise

steps.m: Paradise (as the subject being brought near in وَأُزْلِفَتِ)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is the subject being brought near in the phrase 'And Paradise was brought near'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and ت with shadda between first and second root letters to show someone who does this action regularly
Added ين to make it plural, showing it's talking about multiple people who guard against evil
Added ال to specify these particular people who guard against evil

steps.m: for the ones who guard against (evil)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show these people are receiving or being given something
Basic noun form showing something that is different or other than something else
The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word is describing بَعِيدٍ (far) in the phrase 'not far away', telling us how Paradise is brought near.

steps.m: to be distant or far away

steps.n: Added ي and vowels to change from 'being far' to describing something as 'far away'
The tanwin kasra (ٍ) shows this word is describing غَيْرَ (not) in the phrase
This is the basic attention-getting part that starts many pointing words in Arabic. It's like saying 'look here!'
Adding ذا makes it point to something specific nearby, like pointing with your finger while saying 'this'
The small alif (ٰ) and fatha marks help with pronunciation. In this ayah, it's pointing to what was promised (ما توعدون)

steps.m: that which (object of تُوعَدُونَ - you are promised)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun meaning 'that which' in this context. It refers to what is being promised and connects to the verb تُوعَدُونَ (you are promised) that comes after it.

steps.m: to promise or warn about something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed to passive present tense with 'you' as subject

steps.m: you (all) are being promised/warned (what is mentioned in هٰذَا مَا)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending to show it refers to multiple people. This word refers back to what was just mentioned (هٰذَا مَا - 'this is what')
Added damma and shadda to create a word meaning 'all' or 'every'

steps.m: for every (describing what is promised in تُوعَدُونَ)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction, connecting this word to what was promised in the previous part of the verse

steps.m: to return (especially to Allah in repentance)
one who constantly returns to Allah
steps.n: Added fatha after first letter, doubled the middle letter with shadda, and added alif after it to show this describes someone who does this action repeatedly

steps.m: one who constantly returns to Allah (describing كُلِّ - each/every)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra at the end to show this word is describing كُلِّ (each/every) in the ayah

steps.m: to protect or guard something carefully

steps.n: Adding the basic vowels turns the root into a complete past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this describes someone who does this action intensively

steps.m: a constant guardian/protector (describing أَوَّابٍ - one who frequently returns to Allah)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this word describes أَوَّابٍ (one who frequently returns to Allah) in the ayah
This is a connecting word that means 'whoever' or 'the one who'. In this ayah, it connects to خَشِىَ (feared) to mean 'whoever feared'
The shadda (double letter mark) makes the م stronger to emphasize the meaning

steps.m: he feared respectfully (subject of جَآءَ - he came)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb about one male person. This person is the same one who 'came' (جَآءَ) in this ayah.
Changes from verb 'to be merciful' to noun 'mercy'
Added ان to make it mean 'one who has intense/complete mercy' - like changing 'mercy' to 'extremely merciful'
Added ٱل to make it definite and doubled the ر with shadda to show emphasis

steps.m: The Most Merciful (as the object being feared in مَّنْ خَشِىَ)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is the object being feared in the phrase 'who feared'

steps.m: to be absent or hidden from sight

steps.n: Changes from an action to a thing - from 'being hidden' to 'the hidden/unseen'
Definite article makes the noun definite
Preposition بِ added to indicate 'in/with'

steps.m: in the unseen realm (connected to خَشِىَ - feared)
steps.n: The kasra ending shows this word is connected to the verb خَشِىَ (feared) through the preposition بِ, describing how the fearing happened - in the unseen realm
Added fathas and the middle letter ي changed to ا, which is common for this type of word. This makes it mean 'he came' in the past.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Changed the alif to a long alif (آ) to show emphasis in the coming

steps.m: to turn over, flip, or transform

steps.n: The root letters form a noun meaning 'heart', with tanwin (double dots under) making it 'a heart' instead of 'the heart'

steps.m: with a heart (that belongs to مُّنِيب - one who turns back to Allah)
steps.n: The بِ means 'with'. This heart belongs to someone who turns back to Allah (مُّنِيب) in repentance
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
This form describes someone who habitually or sincerely returns to Allah in repentance
The shadda on مُّ emphasizes the meaning, while the tanween (ٍ) shows this is indefinite (like saying 'a' or 'an' in English)
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he entered' to 'enter!' by adding U- at start and making it a command
Added وا at the end to make it a command to many people
Added ها to show what is being entered, and changed initial alif to wasl form

steps.m: to be safe and at peace

steps.n: Added vowels and alif to make the word mean 'peace' itself rather than the action of being peaceful
